message:
refactor(runtime): flatten advance_region_state recursion to iterative loop

Replace recursive self-calls in advance_region_state with an iterative
`while let Some(region_id) = current.take()` loop. This preserves exact
semantics (Closing→Finalizing re-process, parent advance on child close,
async finalizer break) while bounding stack depth and enabling future
migration to ShardGuard-based locking where recursive mutex acquisition
would deadlock.

Prerequisite for bd-2utc3 (cross-table operation refactor).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

- Note deterministic semantics considerations.

Acceptance:
- Documentation is self-contained and references only current code (no external plan needed).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:57:11.810670058Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-04T20:16:23.876039603Z","closed_at":"2026-02-04T20:16:23.875973811Z","close_reason":"Superseded by bd-3cza0 Docs: runtime locking/sharding notes","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2u2bh","depends_on_id":"bd-2ijqf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-04T19:57:11.818711326Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2un5","title":"Sub-Epic: Platform Reactors - Cross-Platform I/O Support","description":"# Sub-Epic: Platform Reactors

## Overview
Implements native I/O reactors for macOS (kqueue), Windows (IOCP), and Linux epoll
fallback, ensuring Asupersync runs efficiently on all major platforms.

## Background & Motivation
Currently Asupersync only has io_uring support (Linux 5.1+). Anyone saying 'Tokio
works on macOS and Windows' would be right that we don't. This sub-epic fixes that.

## Current State
- io_uring: Implemented (Linux 5.1+)
- epoll: Partial/fallback for older Linux
- kqueue: Not implemented (macOS)
- IOCP: Stub only (Windows)

## Platform Reactor Architecture
```rust
/// Unified reactor trait for all platforms.
pub trait Reactor: Send + Sync {
    /// Register interest in a file descriptor/handle.
    fn register(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd, interest: Interest) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Modify interest for a registered source.
    fn reregister(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd, interest: Interest) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Deregister a source.
    fn deregister(&self, source: &impl AsRawFd) -> io::Result<()>;
    
    /// Wait for events with optional timeout.
    fn poll(&self, events: &mut Events, timeout: Option<Duration>) -> io::Result<usize>;
}

/// Platform-specific reactor selection.
#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    // Try io_uring first, fall back to epoll
    if io_uring::is_available() {
        Ok(Box::new(IoUringReactor::new()?))
    } else {
        Ok(Box::new(EpollReactor::new()?))
    }
}

#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    Ok(Box::new(KqueueReactor::new()?))
}

#[cfg(target_os = "windows")]
pub fn create_reactor() -> io::Result<Box<dyn Reactor>> {
    Ok(Box::new(IocpReactor::new()?))
}
```

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. Reactor trait unification (common interface)
2. kqueue reactor (macOS)
3. IOCP reactor (Windows)
4. epoll fallback (older Linux)
5. Platform reactor E2E tests

## Integration Points
- IoDriver uses platform reactor transparently
- No user-facing API changes
- Lab runtime mock reactor unchanged

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All major platforms supported (Linux, macOS, Windows)
- [ ] Unified Reactor trait for all backends
- [ ] Automatic best-reactor selection per platform
- [ ] No performance regression on Linux io_uring
- [ ] E2E tests pass on all platforms
- [ ] CI matrix includes macOS and Windows","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T01:19:43.413890928Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.013481111Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.013377639Z","close_reason":"All children completed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","platform"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2un5","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2usq","title":"HPACK: recursive decoding stack overflow (FIXED)","description":"# Bug Fix: HPACK Recursive Stack Overflow (COMPLETED)

## Status: FIXED in session 2026-01-31

## Location
`src/http/h2/hpack.rs` - decode_header_block

## Vulnerability
The decoder called itself recursively for dynamic table size updates.
Malicious input with many consecutive size updates could cause stack overflow.

## Fix Applied
Converted to iterative approach with MAX_SIZE_UPDATES limit:

```rust
const MAX_SIZE_UPDATES: usize = 16;

fn decode_header_block(&mut self, data: &[u8]) -> Result<Headers, H2Error> {
    let mut size_updates = 0;
    
    loop {
        if data[pos] & 0xe0 == 0x20 {
            // Dynamic table size update
            size_updates += 1;
            if size_updates > MAX_SIZE_UPDATES {
                return Err(H2Error::compression("too many size updates"));
            }
            // Process size update iteratively
            continue;
        }
        // Normal header processing
        break;
    }
}
```

## Testing Required
- test_dynamic_table_sequence_limit
- Fuzz test with many size updates","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-01T02:56:55.019676791Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T02:57:10.784932772Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T02:57:10.784788645Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2usq","depends_on_id":"bd-1bic","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2utc3","title":"[bd-2ijqf] Cross-Table Operation Refactor (Lock-Order Compliance)","description":"Goal: refactor cross-table operations so all multi-table paths follow the canonical lock order and avoid inversions.

Background:
- Existing code has known inversion risks (from bd-2ijqf notes):
  - `create_obligation`: regions -> obligations
  - `commit_obligation` / `abort_obligation`: obligations -> regions
  - `cancel_request` / `cancel_sibling_tasks`: regions -> tasks
  - `task_completed`: tasks -> obligations -> regions
- Once tables are split, these paths must be refactored to avoid deadlocks and preserve determinism.

Scope / Deliverables:
- Identify all cross-table operations in RuntimeState and rework them to follow `tasks -> regions -> obligations`.
- Where a strict lock order is impossible, use two-phase flows (snapshot IDs under one lock, then apply under another) with explicit invariants.
- Update any helper APIs to ensure callers cannot violate ordering.

Testing & Logging:
- Add unit tests that intentionally exercise these paths (create/commit/abort, cancel flows, task completion) under deterministic scheduling.
- Use lock-order guard assertions (from bd-3gyn2) to ensure no inversions remain.
- Structured logs in tests to show lock acquisition sequence and outcome.

Acceptance Criteria:
- All identified inversion hotspots are refactored and documented.
- No lock-order guard violations in tests.
- Determinism preserved (lab replay equivalence for these flows).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:24.158807564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:44.332407971Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-1tc1m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:34.994520933Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-3gyn2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:44.332371092Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-3xvow","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:40.147507922Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-2utc3","title":"[bd-2ijqf] Cross-Table Operation Refactor (Lock-Order Compliance)","description":"Goal: refactor cross-table operations so all multi-table paths follow the canonical lock order and avoid inversions.

Background:
- Existing code has known inversion risks (from bd-2ijqf notes):
  - `create_obligation`: regions -> obligations
  - `commit_obligation` / `abort_obligation`: obligations -> regions
  - `cancel_request` / `cancel_sibling_tasks`: regions -> tasks
  - `task_completed`: tasks -> obligations -> regions
- Once tables are split, these paths must be refactored to avoid deadlocks and preserve determinism.

Scope / Deliverables:
- Identify all cross-table operations in RuntimeState and rework them to follow `tasks -> regions -> obligations`.
- Where a strict lock order is impossible, use two-phase flows (snapshot IDs under one lock, then apply under another) with explicit invariants.
- Update any helper APIs to ensure callers cannot violate ordering.

Testing & Logging:
- Add unit tests that intentionally exercise these paths (create/commit/abort, cancel flows, task completion) under deterministic scheduling.
- Use lock-order guard assertions (from bd-3gyn2) to ensure no inversions remain.
- Structured logs in tests to show lock acquisition sequence and outcome.

Acceptance Criteria:
- All identified inversion hotspots are refactored and documented.
- No lock-order guard violations in tests.
- Determinism preserved (lab replay equivalence for these flows).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:24.158807564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T18:25:14.877978110Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-1tc1m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:34.994520933Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-3gyn2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:44.332371092Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","depends_on_id":"bd-3xvow","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:32:40.147507922Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":204,"issue_id":"bd-2utc3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SapphireHill: Pre-analysis of cross-table operations for refactoring.

## Lock Order Violations Mapped (state.rs)

Canonical order: B (Regions) → A (Tasks) → C (Obligations)

| Method | Line | Access Order | ShardGuard | Violation |
|--------|------|-------------|------------|-----------|
| create_obligation | 1162 | B→C | for_obligation (B→C) | NONE ✓ |
| commit_obligation | 1247 | C→B | for_obligation (B→C) | C→B ✗ CRITICAL |
| abort_obligation | 1310 | C→B | for_obligation (B→C) | C→B ✗ CRITICAL |
| task_completed | 1901 | A→C→B | for_task_completed (B→A→C) | A→C→B ✗ CRITICAL |
| cancel_request | 1626 | B→A→B | for_cancel (B→A) | B→A→B ✗ CRITICAL |
| cancel_sibling_tasks | 1561 | B→A | for_cancel (B→A) | B→A ✗ MEDIUM |
| advance_region_state | 2216 | B→A→C→B | for_task_completed (B→A→C) | RECURSIVE ✗ HIGH |
| can_region_complete_close | 2170 | B→A | for_cancel (B→A) | B→A ✗ MEDIUM |

## ShardGuard Already Handles Ordering

The guards acquire locks upfront in canonical order:
- for_obligation: B→C (fixes commit/abort_obligation)
- for_task_completed: B→A→C (fixes task_completed)
- for_cancel: B→A (fixes cancel_request/cancel_sibling_tasks)

## Refactoring Strategy Per Method

### 1. commit_obligation / abort_obligation (CRITICAL)
Current: accesses obligations first, then regions (C→B).
Fix: Under for_obligation guard (B→C), both tables are already locked. Rewrite to take &RegionTable and &ObligationTable as params instead of &mut self. Region access (resolve_obligation + advance_region_state) happens through the guard.

### 2. task_completed (CRITICAL)
Current: task removal → obligation scan → region update (A→C→B).
Fix: Under for_task_completed guard (B→A→C), all three locked. Restructure to:
  1. Read task record, extract owner/waiters (A)
  2. Remove task (A)
  3. Scan/abort orphaned obligations (C)
  4. Remove task from region, advance state (B)
No ordering issue since guard holds all three.

### 3. cancel_request / cancel_sibling_tasks (CRITICAL)
Current: collects regions, iterates tasks, re-accesses regions (B→A→B).
Fix: Under for_cancel guard (B→A), both locked. The re-access of regions is fine since it's the same guard. Just ensure methods take &RegionTable + &TaskTable.

### 4. advance_region_state (HARDEST - RECURSIVE)
Current: B→A→C→B with recursive self-calls.
Fix options:
  a) ITERATIVE LOOP: Replace recursion with a work queue of region IDs to advance. Single for_task_completed guard covers all three tables.
  b) TWO-PHASE: Phase 1 (under guard) gathers decisions. Phase 2 (new guard) executes.
  
RECOMMENDATION: Option (a) - iterative loop. The recursion only happens when:
  - Closing/Draining → Finalizing (line 2249)
  - Parent advance after child close (line 2299)
  
Convert to: let mut work = vec\![region_id]; while let Some(rid) = work.pop() { ... if transition { work.push(rid); } if closed { work.push(parent_id); } }

### 5. handle_obligation_leaks (MEDIUM-HIGH)
Current: calls abort_obligation (C→B) in Recover mode.
Fix: Under for_task_completed guard (B→A→C), all locked. No ordering issue.

## Key Principle
The ShardGuard design means methods no longer need to worry about acquisition order - the guard has already acquired everything needed. The refactoring is about changing method signatures from &mut self (single RuntimeState lock) to operating on guard fields (&RegionTable, &TaskTable, &ObligationTable).

## Deadlock Scenarios (Current Code, Single Lock = Safe)
These only become real when we switch from single Mutex<RuntimeState> to ShardedState:
1. Concurrent commit_obligation (C) + cancel_request (B→A) → 3-way if anything holds A→C
2. task_completed (A→C→B) + cancel_request (B→A) → circular wait
3. advance_region_state recursion → nested non-monotonic lock ordering

All resolved by guard-first approach.

## Implementation Order Recommendation
1. Flatten advance_region_state recursion → iterative loop (hardest, do first)
2. Refactor commit/abort_obligation to take table refs
3. Refactor task_completed to use guard pattern
4. Refactor cancel_request/cancel_sibling_tasks
5. Wire up to ShardGuard-based callers","created_at":"2026-02-05T18:25:14Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2vik","title":"Sub-Epic: HTTP/3 (QUIC) - Next-Generation Web Transport","description":"# Sub-Epic: HTTP/3 (QUIC) - Next-Generation Web Transport

## Overview
Implement HTTP/3 over QUIC with full Cx integration, providing next-generation
web transport with improved performance and cancel-correct semantics.

## Background & Motivation
HTTP/3 uses QUIC instead of TCP, providing:
- Multiplexed streams without head-of-line blocking
- Built-in TLS 1.3
- Connection migration (IP changes)
- 0-RTT connection establishment
- Better performance on lossy networks

When someone says "Tokio has HTTP/3", we respond: "So do we, with proper QUIC 
cancellation semantics."

## Strategy: Wrap quinn

The quinn crate provides a mature QUIC implementation. We'll wrap it with Cx
integration rather than implementing QUIC from scratch.

## Architecture

### QUIC Layer (wrap quinn)
```rust
pub struct QuicEndpoint {
    inner: quinn::Endpoint,
}

impl QuicEndpoint {
    /// Create a QUIC endpoint.
    pub fn new(cx: &Cx, config: QuicConfig) -> Outcome<Self, QuicError>;
    
    /// Connect to a server.
    pub async fn connect(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
        addr: SocketAddr,
        server_name: &str,
    ) -> Outcome<QuicConnection, QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept incoming connections.
    pub async fn accept(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<QuicConnection, QuicError>;
}

pub struct QuicConnection {
    inner: quinn::Connection,
}

impl QuicConnection {
    /// Open a bidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_bi(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError>;
    
    /// Open a unidirectional stream.
    pub async fn open_uni(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<SendStream, QuicError>;
    
    /// Accept an incoming stream.
    pub async fn accept_bi(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<(SendStream, RecvStream), QuicError>;
}
```

### HTTP/3 Layer
```rust
pub struct H3Client {
    conn: QuicConnection,
}

impl H3Client {
    /// Create HTTP/3 client from QUIC connection.
    pub async fn new(cx: &Cx, conn: QuicConnection) -> Outcome<Self, H3Error>;
    
    /// Send HTTP request.
    pub async fn request(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
        req: Request,
    ) -> Outcome<Response, H3Error>;
}

pub struct H3Server {
    endpoint: QuicEndpoint,
}

impl H3Server {
    /// Accept HTTP/3 connection.
    pub async fn accept(&self, cx: &Cx) -> Outcome<H3Connection, H3Error>;
}
```

## Tasks in This Sub-Epic
1. QUIC endpoint wrapper (wrap quinn with Cx)
2. QUIC connection and streams
3. HTTP/3 client implementation
4. HTTP/3 server implementation  
5. HTTP/3 E2E tests

## Cancellation Semantics
- QUIC connections gracefully closed on Cx cancel
- Streams reset with STOP_SENDING on cancel
- No data loss for completed frames
- Connection migration preserved if possible

## Integration Points
- Uses existing TLS infrastructure
- Integrates with IoDriver for UDP
- Works with existing HTTP types (Request, Response)

## Dependencies
- Requires: QUIC implementation (quinn crate)
- Requires: HTTP/3 framing (h3 crate)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] QUIC client connects to HTTP/3 servers
- [ ] QUIC server accepts HTTP/3 connections
- [ ] Stream multiplexing works correctly
- [ ] 0-RTT resumption supported
- [ ] Cancellation triggers proper stream reset
- [ ] Lab runtime tests deterministic
- [ ] Interop with major browsers/servers

## References
- RFC 9000: QUIC Transport
- RFC 9114: HTTP/3
- quinn: https://github.com/quinn-rs/quinn
- h3: https://github.com/hyperium/h3","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:21:41.951249982Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.042135333Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T06:50:53.042018105Z","close_reason":"All children completed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ecosystem-parity","http","quic"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2vik","depends_on_id":"bd-1gb7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:10Z","created_by":"import"}]}
